role_summary: Co-founder (with Bobby Seale) of the Black Panther Party for Self-Defense in Oakland, October 1966; theorist of "revolutionary suicide"; the BPP's Minister of Defense.

photo.alt: Huey P. Newton, Black Panther Minister of Defense, 1968 poster
photo.photographer: Blair Stapp (original photograph); Black Panther Party (poster design)
photo.repository: Wikimedia Commons (object held by Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture, accession 2011.58)
photo.license: Public domain (U.S., published 1931-1977 without copyright notice)

ai_reading: Three corpus voices discuss Newton, the smallest external-figure footprint in the catalog: Elmer Dixon (entry 37, Seattle BPP co-founder), Aaron Dixon (entry 1, Seattle BPP captain), and Kathleen Cleaver (entry 73, BPP Communications Secretary). All three are BPP-cohort voices, and all three sit at the Nonviolence-as-Theology pole of the catalog's nonviolence axis (the catalog's documented AI-vs-culture inversion). The embedding therefore reads Newton as discussed only within the BPP's own internal-organizational vocabulary, never crossing into the SCLC/SNCC/NAACP transcripts the catalog separately tracks, consistent with his cultural placement as the BPP-internal theorist whose ideas did not circulate widely outside the party.
biographical_paragraph: Huey P. Newton is discussed in this corpus by 3 interviewees across the catalog (Elmer Dixon (entry 37), Aaron Dixon (entry 1), Kathleen Cleaver (entry 73)), but was not interviewed for the Civil Rights History Project; this catalog page treats Huey P. Newton as an external figure whose presence in the archive is mediated through other voices. The canonical scholarly source the catalog cites for biographical orientation appears as [src: 1] below.

sources.title: Huey P. Newton, Revolutionary Suicide
sources.publisher: Harcourt Brace Jovanovich, 1973 (reprinted Penguin Classics, 2009); WorldCat OCLC 800129
